definition.definition: To describe or indicate precisely; to outline the exact boundaries or features of something.
usage_notes: Used when precision and clear boundaries matter
Common in technical, legal, and academic writing
Can be used both literally (drawing outlines) and figuratively (defining roles)

etymology.origin_summary: From Latin 'delineare' — to sketch out. 'de-' (completely) + 'lineare' (to draw a line), from 'linea' (line).

examples: The contract clearly delineates the responsibilities of each party.
She delineated the project scope to prevent scope creep.

confusions.difference: 'Describe' gives a general account; 'delineate' implies precise, boundary-level detail.
'Define' states meaning; 'delineate' maps out edges and distinctions.

definition.definition: Expressed clearly and in few words; brief but comprehensive.
usage_notes: Positive connotation — implies efficiency, not lack of depth
Valued in professional communication, technical writing, and presentations

etymology.origin_summary: From Latin 'succinctus' — tucked up, girded. 'sub-' (under) + 'cingere' (to gird). Metaphor of being tightly bound.

examples: His succinct summary captured the key points in under a minute.
Keep your emails succinct — busy people appreciate brevity.

confusions.difference: Nearly synonymous, but 'succinct' emphasizes compression; 'concise' emphasizes removal of excess.
'Terse' can imply rudeness or abruptness; 'succinct' is always positive.
